turns.user_message: My wife, actually. She's been on my case for years, saying I'm going to get sick and die if I don't stop. But I always just tell her not to worry, that I'll live longer than her anyway. (chuckles) But I guess she finally got me tired of arguing about it, so now I'm here.
step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?

Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.

There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.

- Known triggers: slip up, going back to smoking, morning craving
- Tried strategies: tried quitting → did not help, chewing gum, taking a walk, going to the back of the shop and straightening up the shelves
[Clinical Knowledge]
- If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

- If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
- Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.
[Recent Conversation]

Therapist: You're saying that you're aware of your morning craving after tea and are considering replacing it with chewing gum or taking a walk. What do you think about trying to do something immediately after you finish your tea, like a specific action that becomes a new habit?

Therapist: You're feeling a bit skeptical about trying out a new habit, but you're open to giving it a shot. Therapist: The patient is feeling more open to trying a new habit, especially one that's simple and purposeful, like doing something right after finishing their tea. What do you think about sharing your idea with a family member or friend, so they can support you in making a change?
